text: The robotic instruments are used to repair a recurrent inguinal hernia and remove the previously placed mesh plug from the surgical site.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum a few centimeters above the mesh plug location and raises a peritoneal flap using a combination of sharp, blunt, and electrocautery dissection techniques.
The surgeon reduces the lipoma of the spermatic cord, visibly retracting the fatty tissue away from the surrounding structures.
The surgeon focuses on separating the mesh plug from the surrounding tissues at the deep inguinal ring using robotic instruments.
The surgeon continues the dissection of the hernia sac, fully reducing it from the surrounding tissues.
The surgeon continues dissection around the right Cooper's ligament within the space of Retzius, carefully separating the ligament from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon reduces a small lipoma from the femoral canal, carefully manipulating the tissue to remove the fatty mass.
The surgeon focuses on the mesh plug, which is closely adhered to the surrounding soft tissues. Careful and tedious dissection is performed to separate the plug from these tissues.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers around the testicular vessels and vas deferens, ensuring these structures remain intact during the dissection process.
The testicular vessels are clearly visible in the surgical field, with surrounding tissues carefully dissected to avoid injury.
The video shows the intimate contact between the testicular vessels on the right side of the image, with the vas deferens visible to the left.
The surgeon completes the removal of the plug and proceeds to dissect the peritoneum proximally, creating ample space for the placement of the mesh.
A 10x15 cm self-gripping mesh is placed in the preperitoneal space, centered over the hernia defect, and carefully unrolled.
The surgeon positions the mesh to ensure adequate coverage over potential hernia spaces, including the deep inguinal ring, Hasselbach's triangle, and the femoral canal.

The surgeon achieves adequate positioning of the mesh in the preperitoneal space and proceeds to close the peritoneal flap using a barb suture, effectively separating the mesh from the intra-abdominal organs.
The surgeon closes a small hole in the peritoneum, which was created where the peritoneum adhered to the plug, using redundant hernia sac tissue as a buttress.
